data.title: Claude source code leak: A butterfly effect triggered by a .map file
data.description:
Written by: Claude

I. Origin

In the early morning of March 31, 2026, a tweet caused a huge uproar in the developer community.

Chaofan Shou, an intern at a blockchain security company, discovered that the Anthropic official npm package included a source map file that exposed the complete source code of Claude Code to the public internet. He immediately published this discovery on X, along with a direct download link.

This post exploded like a signal flare in the developer community. Within hours, more than 512,000 lines of TypeScript code were mirrored to GitHub and analyzed in real time by thousands of developers.

This was the second major data breach incident that occurred at Anthropic in less than a week.

Just five days ago (March 26th), a CMS configuration error at Anthropic led to the public release of nearly 3,000 internal files, including a draft blog post about the upcoming "Claude Mythos" model. II. How did the leak happen? The technical reason for this incident is ironically absurd—the root cause was an incorrect inclusion of a source map file (.map file) in the npm package. These files are used to map compressed and obfuscated production code back to the original source code, facilitating debugging and locating error lines. This .map file contained a link to a zip archive in Anthropic's own Cloudflare R2 bucket. Shou and other developers simply downloaded this zip archive without any hacking. The file was there, completely public. The version that was affected was v2.1.88 of @anthropic-ai/claude-code, which included a 59.8MB JavaScript source map file. In a statement to The Register, Anthropic acknowledged that "an earlier version of Claude Code also experienced a similar source code leak in February 2025." This means the same error occurred twice within 13 months. Ironically, Claude Code has an internal system called "Undercover Mode," specifically designed to prevent Anthropic's internal codenames from being accidentally leaked in git commits… and the engineers then packaged the entire source code into a .map file. Another contributing factor to the incident may be the toolchain itself: Anthropic acquired Bun at the end of the year, and Claude Code is built on top of Bun. On March 11, 2026, a bug report (#28001) was submitted to the Bun's issue tracking system, pointing out that the Bun was still generating and outputting source maps in production mode, contradicting the official documentation. This issue remains open. Anthropic's official response was brief and restrained: "No user data or credentials were involved or leaked. This was a human error during the release packaging process, not a security vulnerability. We are working on measures to prevent such incidents from happening again." III. What was leaked? Code Size The leaked content covers approximately 1900 files and over 500,000 lines of code. This is not model weights, but the entire "software layer" engineering implementation of Claude Code—including the tool invocation framework, multi-agent orchestration, permission system, memory system, and other core architectures.

Unreleased Feature Roadmap

This is the most strategically valuable part of the leak.

KAIROS Autonomous Daemon: This feature codename, mentioned over 150 times, comes from the ancient Greek word for "appropriate time," representing a fundamental shift in Claude Code towards a "resident background agent." KAIROS includes a process called autoDream, which performs "memory consolidation" when the user is idle—merging fragmented observations, eliminating logical inconsistencies, and solidifying vague insights into deterministic facts. When the user returns, the agent's context is clean and highly relevant.

Internal Model Codenames and Performance Data: The leak confirms that Capybara is the internal codename for a Claude 4.6 variant, Fennec corresponds to Opus 4.6, and the unreleased Numbat is still in testing.

KAIROS Autonomous Daemon: This feature codename, mentioned over 150 times, comes from the ancient Greek word for "appropriate time," representing a fundamental shift in Claude Code towards a "resident background agent." KAIROS includes a process called autoDream, which performs "memory consolidation" when the user is idle—merging fragmented observations, eliminating logical inconsistencies, and solidifying vague insights into deterministic facts. When the user returns, the agent's context is clean and highly relevant.

Internal Model Codenames and Performance Data: The leak confirms that Capybara is the internal codename for a Claude 4.6 variant, Fennec corresponds to Opus 4.6, and Numbat, which has not yet been released, is still in testing.

The code comments also reveal that Capybara v8 has a spurious statement rate of 29-30%, a regression from v4's 16.7%. Anti-Distillation Mechanism: The code contains a feature flag called ANTI_DISTILLATION_CC. When enabled, Claude Code injects fake tool definitions into API requests, aiming to pollute API traffic data that competitors might use for model training. **Beta API Feature List:** The `constants/betas.ts` file reveals all the beta features negotiated between Claude Code and the API, including a 1 million token context window (`context-1m-2025-08-07`), AFK mode (`afk-mode-2026-01-31`), task budget management (`task-budgets-2026-03-13`), and a series of other undisclosed capabilities. **Embedded Pokémon-Style Virtual Partner System:** The code even contains a complete virtual partner system (Buddy), including species rarity, shiny variants, procedurally generated attributes, and a "soul description" written by Claude during the initial incubation. Partner types are determined by a deterministic pseudo-random number generator based on the user ID hash, and the same user always gets the same partner.

IV. Concurrent Supply Chain Attacks

This incident did not occur in isolation. Within the same time window as the source code leak, the axios package on npm suffered a separate supply chain attack.

Between 00:21 and 03:29 UTC on March 31, 2026, installing or updating Claude Code via npm may have inadvertently introduced a malicious version (axios 1.14.1 or 0.30.4) containing a Remote Access Trojan (RAT).

Anthropic recommends that affected developers consider their hosts completely compromised, rotate all keys, and reinstall the operating system.

The overlap in the timing of these two incidents makes the situation even more chaotic and dangerous.

V. Impact on the Industry Direct Damage to Anthropic For a company with annualized revenue of $19 billion and in a period of rapid growth, this leak is not merely a security oversight, but a significant loss of strategic intellectual property. At least part of Claude Code's capabilities do not originate from the underlying large language model itself, but rather from the software "framework" built around the model—which guides the model on how to use tools and provides crucial guardrails and instructions to regulate model behavior. These guardrails and instructions are now clearly visible to competitors.

A Warning for the Entire AI Agent Tool Ecosystem

This leak won't sink Anthropic, but it provides all competitors with a free engineering textbook—how to build production-grade AI programming agents, and which tool directions are worth focusing on.

The real value of the leaked content lies not in the code itself, but in the product roadmap revealed by the functional markers. KAIROS, the backdistillation mechanism—these are strategic details that competitors can now anticipate and react to preemptively. Code can be refactored, but strategic surprises, once leaked, cannot be recovered.

VI. Profound Implications for Agent Coding

This leak is a mirror, reflecting several core propositions of current AI Agent engineering:

1. The capability boundaries of an agent are largely determined by the "framework layer," not the model itself.

The exposure of 500,000 lines of Claude Code reveals a fact that is meaningful to the entire industry: the same underlying model, coupled with different tool orchestration frameworks, memory management mechanisms, and permission systems, will produce drastically different agent capabilities.

This means that "whose model is the strongest" is no longer the only competitive dimension—"whose framework engineering is more sophisticated" is equally crucial. 2. Long-range autonomy is the next core battleground. The existence of the KAIROS daemon indicates that the next stage of competition in the industry will focus on "enabling agents to work effectively even without supervision." Background memory integration, cross-session knowledge transfer, and autonomous reasoning during idle periods—once these capabilities mature, they will fundamentally change the basic model of agent-human collaboration. 3. Back-distillation and intellectual property protection will become new fundamental topics in AI engineering. Anthropic has implemented a back-distillation mechanism at the code level, indicating the emergence of a new engineering field: how to prevent competitors from using one's own AI system for training data collection. This is not only a technical issue but will also evolve into a new battleground for legal and commercial competition. 4. Supply Chain Security is the Achilles' Heel of AI Tools When AI programming tools are distributed through public package managers like npm, they are vulnerable to supply chain attacks, just like other open-source software. The unique aspect of AI tools is that once a backdoor is implanted, attackers gain not only code execution rights but also deep penetration of the entire development workflow. 5. The More Complex the System, the Greater the Need for Automated Release Guards "A misconfigured .npmignore or the files field in package.json can expose everything." For any team building an AI Agent product, this lesson shouldn't have to come at such a high price—introducing automated release content review in the CI/CD pipeline should be standard practice, not a reactive measure. Epilogue Today is April 1st, 2026, April Fool's Day. But this is not a joke. Anthropic made the same mistake twice in thirteen months. The source code had been mirrored globally, and DMCA deletion requests couldn't keep up with the forking speed. The product roadmap, which should have been deeply buried within the intranet, was now everyone's reference material. For Anthropic, this was a painful lesson. For the entire industry, it was an unexpected moment of transparency—allowing us a glimpse into how the most advanced AI programming agents are built line by line.

data.title: Is quantum cryptic cracking nearing reality? Google issues warnings – what does this mean for encryption?
data.description:
Written by: jsai@Jinse Finance

Is the quantum computer really coming?

Recently, Google issued two blog posts warning of the imminent arrival of the quantum era, urging various industries to seriously address the quantum threat.

Key points of Google's two announcements

I. Establishing a timeline for post-quantum cryptography migration, aiming to complete it by 2029

On March 25, 2026, Google officially announced in a blog post that it has significantly moved up the deadline for its internal post-quantum cryptography (PQC) migration to the end of 2029.

Regarding the reasons for the earlier announcement, Google stated that based on its own progress in quantum computing (including the breakthrough in quantum error correction technology with the Willow 105 qubit chip), the rapid development of quantum hardware, the optimization of error correction algorithms, and the significant reduction in the estimated resources for quantum factorization (for example, the number of qubits required to break a 2048-bit RSA has decreased from the previously estimated 20 million to about 1 million), it believes that the quantum Q-Day threat is coming sooner than originally expected. Q-Day refers to the point at which a quantum computer can effectively break current mainstream public-key encryption systems (RSA and elliptic curve cryptography ECC/ECDSA). Once this happens, existing encryption will face the risk of being "instantly cracked," affecting almost all confidential data in banks, governments, the military, and the internet. Google stated that quantum computers will pose a significant threat to current encryption standards, especially encryption and digital signatures. The threats to encryption are already apparent, such as "store-first, decrypt-later" attacks; while digital signatures represent a future threat, requiring a transition to PQC before deployment to a Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC). Therefore, Google has adjusted its threat model, prioritizing the migration of authentication services to PQC—a crucial component of cybersecurity and digital signature migration. II. Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computers (CRQC) Are Approaching Reality A blog post and accompanying white paper published on March 31, 2026, indicate that future quantum computers may require fewer qubits and quantum gates than ever before to break elliptic curve cryptography used to protect cryptocurrencies and other systems. In the white paper, Google shares updated estimates of the quantum computing "resources" (i.e., qubits and quantum gates) required to break the 256-bit Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP-256, the foundation of elliptic curve cryptography). Google uses the number of logical qubits (error-correcting qubits consisting of hundreds of physical qubits) and Tooffoli gates (expensive basic operations performed on qubits, a major driver of execution time for many algorithms) to represent resource estimates. Specifically, two quantum circuits (a series of quantum gates) implementing Shor's algorithm were written to crack ECDLP-256: one circuit uses fewer than 1200 logical qubits and 90 million Tooffoli gates, and the other uses fewer than 1450 logical qubits and 70 million Tooffoli gates. Google estimates that, under the assumption that hardware performance meets the standards of some of Google's flagship quantum processors, these circuits can execute in minutes using superconducting qubit CRQC with fewer than 500,000 physical qubits. This is approximately 20 times fewer physical qubits than required to solve ECDLP-256. Google stated that with the continuous advancement of science and technology, cryptography-related quantum computers (CRQC) are gradually approaching reality, necessitating a transition to PQC. This is the reason Google recently released its 2029 migration timeline. These two Google blog posts mark the tech giant's formal shift of the quantum threat from a "theoretical vision" to a "close-up engineering task." What does this mean for BTC and ETH? The core signature algorithms of Bitcoin and Ethereum are both ECDSA (based on secp256k1 elliptic curves), and public keys are typically exposed in transactions/addresses (especially in traditional addresses and some Taproot scenarios). Once quantum computers reach cryptographically correlated quantum computing (CRQC), they can reverse-engineer private keys from public keys, enabling the theft of funds or the forgery of transactions. This poses a direct threat to addresses with exposed public keys ("harvest now, decrypt later" risk). The common impacts include: 1. Fund security risk: Cold wallets/unspent UTXOs are relatively secure if the public key is not exposed, but most on-chain assets are exposed. After Q-Day, addresses that have not been upgraded may be stolen by quantum attacks. 2. Trust and adoption impact: Institutional investors may avoid chains that are "quantum insecure," triggering capital outflows or price volatility. 3. "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later": Attackers can collect encrypted data/public keys now and crack them later when quantum computers mature. While both Bitcoin and Ethereum face common threats, their responses differ significantly. This may be rooted in their community cultures. BTC faces higher risks and slower responses. The Bitcoin community is more conservative and highly decentralized; upgrades require network-wide consensus (soft fork/hard fork), and currently lack a coordinated roadmap. Google's move is seen as a wake-up call for Bitcoin developers; many Bitcoin community members still believe 2029 is too aggressive and the quantum threat is exaggerated, but Google's hardware progress has led some to reassess. Current status: No official network-wide PQC migration plan; progress is slow. Proposals such as BIP 360 provide partial quantum protection for Taproot's critical path costs, but a complete upgrade still requires community consensus. Possible paths: Introduce new opcode/address types via a soft fork to support PQC signatures; or develop a quantum-safe wallet/address format. Some developers are already discussing this, but the decentralized nature makes coordination difficult and time-sensitive. Challenges: The Bitcoin community is deeply divided (many believe that quantum computers cannot reach scale in the short term), and if no action is taken before 2029, it may face an "existential threat" and a loss of institutional confidence. ETH is relatively better prepared; a roadmap has been developed. Ethereum has been laying the groundwork for 8 years, and the Ethereum Foundation (EF) recently released its Post-Quantum Ethereum roadmap, which is highly aligned with Google's 2029 timeline. EF believes that quantum "cryptography-related" threats may occur in 8-12 years, but "work must begin now." Roadmap: EF has launched a dedicated website (pq.ethereum.org) and a "Strawmap"/four-phase (or seven hard fork) roadmap, aiming to complete the Layer 1 protocol-level upgrade (including validator signatures, accounts, data storage, and proof system) by 2029. A full migration of the execution layer may take several more years. Specific Measures: Use STARK-based or PQC signatures; implement gradually through multiple hard forks (such as "I", "J", etc.); testnet is already running. Vitalik Buterin and others have publicly discussed quantum protection multiple times. Advantages: Smart contract platform upgrades are more flexible, with a clear timeline and resources, and progress far exceeds that of Bitcoin. Conclusion: Regardless of whether it's BTC or ETH, users should pay attention to quantum-safe wallets (such as hardware wallets that support PQC signatures) as soon as possible and migrate their assets after the protocol upgrade. Google's announcement has accelerated the quantum protection process across the entire crypto industry—Ethereum is already taking action, and Bitcoin needs to follow suit as soon as possible, otherwise the risks will be significantly amplified. In the coming years, PQC compatibility will become a standard feature of blockchains. If quantum hardware continues to advance at the current pace, 2029 will be a significant watershed year in the history of cryptocurrency security.

data.title: Delphi Digital: Stablecoins – The Next Frontier in Financial Infrastructure
data.description:
Author: Delphi Digital, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Stablecoin issuers have now become the 19th largest holders of US Treasury bonds, and their assets are exactly the same as the underlying assets supporting ordinary savings accounts. One of the most important issues in current cryptocurrency legislation is how these institutions are allowed to dispose of their Treasury bond yields.

Yield Gap

There is a huge interest rate differential between US Treasury bond yields and the deposit rates that banks pay to depositors. Treasury bond yields remain in the 3.5%–4% range, while the average yield of ordinary savings accounts is only 0.39%.

Stablecoin issuers hold the same underlying assets. The GENIUS Act prohibits them from distributing yields directly to holders, and regulators are expanding the scope of this ban to include various circumvention methods that have emerged since then.

If people can hold funds that are readily liquidated, have no credit risk, and generate interest, their willingness to keep cash in traditional bank accounts will decrease significantly.

Critics characterize this as a national security issue.

On a practical level, it poses a threat to the fractional-reserve financing model upon which the entire credit system depends. Structural financing problems do exist; banks rely on deposits to issue loans. If a large influx of dollars into fully-reserve stablecoins invested solely in government bonds occurs, private credit creation will contract. Funds will shift to sovereign assets, and the credit transmission mechanism supporting SME lending and consumer credit will begin to fail.

Small and medium-sized banks and credit unions will be the first to be affected. They rely on deposits far more than large institutions to support their lending businesses. Once these deposits flow onto the blockchain, they will not only lose interest rate spreads but also their corresponding lending capacity.

This is the real core contradiction in the stablecoin legislation debate: End users can obtain lower-cost, faster-circulating US dollars, but the credit system will lose the deposit base needed to maintain lending.

This is precisely the core contradiction in the stablecoin legislation debate: End users can obtain lower-cost, faster-circulating US dollars, but the credit system will lose the deposit base needed to maintain lending.

Everyone Wants to Be a Bank. Over the past few months, fintech companies and crypto businesses have been vying for banking licenses by directly applying for licenses or acquiring existing banks. Following the global financial crisis, the US virtually stopped issuing commercial banking licenses for nearly a decade. The number of new banks established plummeted from approximately 132 per year to just 6. When license resources dried up, the market stopped trying to become a bank and instead built systems around banks; now, the situation is completely reversed. For crypto-native companies and stablecoin issuers, a federal license means access to the Federal Reserve's instant payment systems FedNow and Fedwire, and direct control over the clearing and settlement layer. For traditional fintech companies, this means breaking free from dependence on third-party banks and achieving full-chain vertical integration. This trend of convergence is progressing in both directions. In December 2025, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) passed a proposed rule allowing state banks under its supervision to issue payment-type stablecoins through subsidiaries. In February 2026, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) followed suit, introducing similar proposed rules for national banks. Once these rules are finalized, any licensed depository institution will have a legitimate channel to issue stablecoins. The End of Vertical Integration: Stripe is the most typical example. Through acquisitions of Bridge, Privy, and Metronome, and its partnership with Paradigm to create Tempo, it is building a complete payment infrastructure from stablecoin issuance to merchant settlement.

It's not just them that are moving in this direction. The stablecoin technology architecture highly corresponds to the institutional financial system: blockchain settlement replaces the Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS) system, stablecoin issuance replaces commercial bank deposits, on-chain liquidity and foreign exchange trading replace agency banking services, compliance logic replaces anti-money laundering (AML) infrastructure, and various payment applications are built on top of all of this.

Major institutions in the fintech, banking, and crypto sectors are all trying to control as many levels of ecosystem discourse power as possible.

**Issuer Risk Becomes a New Type of Credit Risk** Tether and Circle together account for over 84% of the total stablecoin market capitalization. The brief de-pegging of USDC during the Silicon Valley Bank bankruptcy vividly demonstrated this **concentration risk**. Although Circle was solvent and had sufficient reserves, some of its funds were temporarily trapped in troubled banks and could not be accessed. In the traditional banking system, payment risk is distributed among various institutions, while credit risk is distributed across the credit assets of thousands of banks. However, in the stablecoin system, the settlement risk between participants is eliminated, but it is concentrated at the issuer level. The system does not achieve zero risk; rather, the risk has been vertically transferred—from dispersed counterparty exposure to highly concentrated issuer-dependent risk. The issuance of white-label stablecoins is expanding, with institutions such as Paxos, Agora, Brale, M0, and Bridge all offering issuance-as-a-service. Over the past year, Paxos, through partnerships with companies like PayPal, has seen its market capitalization increase nearly eightfold, with partners handling distribution while Paxos focuses on infrastructure and compliance. While this model has proven viable, it hasn't yet shaken the dominance of existing leading institutions. Such a level of liquidity inherently possesses a strong self-reinforcing effect. Conclusion: In just a few years, stablecoins have grown from niche trading tools into a crucial infrastructure for cross-border remittances in emerging markets. The next wave of impact will directly target the core financing model of the banking system itself. The GENIUS Act has established a regulatory framework, and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) is currently developing implementation rules, with a finalization scheduled for July 2026. The remaining key variable is whether the CLARITY Act will pass. If approved, it will directly determine whether various platforms can offer any form of stablecoin yield.

data.title: Why does Wu Jihan prefer manual labor when his peers seek patronage from industry giants?
data.description:
On March 30, 2026, Bitdeer released a stunning set of Nordic-style remodeling blueprints that shocked the market. They announced that their subsidiary, TDC, has commissioned Norwegian contractor DCI to transform an existing facility in Tydal, Norway, into a 180 MW AI data center, expected to be completed in December 2026. The center will primarily run NVIDIA's latest Vera Rubin AI platform. But this isn't a story of money talks. Bitdeer's total debt has already exceeded $1.3 billion. On one hand, there's the ledger nearing its red line; on the other, there's the massive investment in top-tier European computing infrastructure. What's the real motive behind this stark contrast? The root of all these actions lies in the precipitous decline of BitDeer's old business. In April 2024, Bitcoin underwent its fourth halving, reducing the block reward from 6.25 to 3.125. Revenue was instantly halved, but electricity costs and equipment depreciation remained unchanged. In the fourth quarter of 2025, BitDeer's gross profit margin for its mining business dropped to 4.7%. This means that for every 100 yuan earned, less than 5 yuan remained after deducting costs. Even more serious was the fact that other fronts were also starting to succumb to the pandemic. The third-quarter report released on November 10, 2025, showed that the company's self-developed A4 chip was significantly delayed, resulting in a loss per share of US$1.28, far exceeding the expected loss of US$0.22. The stock price fell by 14.9% the following day. Immediately following, on the afternoon of November 11th, a fire broke out at its mine under construction in Ohio, causing two buildings to collapse. To make matters worse, starting in December 2025, multiple law firms filed class-action lawsuits, accusing the company of misappropriating funds from June 6, 2024 to November 10, 2025, regarding A4. Misleading statements regarding the technological maturity and mass production timeline of the chips. Profits are being squeezed to the extreme, and the core business is facing constant turmoil. With cash flow already so tight, why make such a large-scale foray into Norway? II. The most valuable asset isn't the mining machines, but cheap electricity. Because going to Norway is actually about finding a breakthrough. BitDeer announced on March 30, 2026, that it would transform its existing 175 MW mining facility in Tydal, Norway, into a 180 MW AI data center. "AI server rooms are most susceptible to heat and consume the most electricity." An industry insider pointed out, "The industrial electricity price in Norway is approximately $0.04-0.09 per kilowatt-hour, and the average annual temperature is about 5.8°C, essentially acting as a natural air conditioner." With this natural environment, their liquid cooling system can achieve a PUE of 1.1, meaning it costs very little. Of the 1 yuan electricity bill, more than 90 cents were used for real work. Furthermore, the local electricity is almost 100% hydroelectric, and the waste heat from the server room can be transferred to nearby food factories. This kind of "green and environmentally friendly" story is very popular in the capital market. In February 2026, Bitdeer issued $325 million in convertible bonds at an interest rate of 5%. With natural air conditioning and cheap electricity, converting suitable mining farms into AI data centers has become an industry-wide consensus. However, BitDeer has chosen an extremely ambitious path in how to actually do it. By 2026, its competitors were frantically trying to find new solutions. People either become asset-light landlords or seek stability by partnering with tech giants. In June 2024, US-listed mining company Core Scientific signed a 12-year computing power contract worth over $10 billion with a cloud service provider. In December 2025, Canadian mining company Hut 8 signed a $7 billion AI infrastructure agreement with Google as a guarantor. In January 2026, Riot sold 1080 Bitcoins to acquire land in Texas, and then signed a data center lease with AMD with a potential value of $1 billion. However, Wu Jihan, who came from the Bitmain era, still maintained his typical personal style: raising funds, building, and operating the facilities himself. This allows them to fully capitalize on the profits of both upstream and downstream businesses, but it also means shouldering the enormous risks associated with heavy assets all by themselves. As of February 2026, Bitdeer's AI business was running 2096 GPUs with a utilization rate of around 64%, generating approximately $21 million in revenue annually. On the other hand, the over $1.3 billion debt generates $65 million in interest annually. The current AI revenue is only enough to cover about four months of interest. This heavy asset-heavy blueprint has directly divided Wall Street into two sharply contrasting camps.

IV. Nearly 40% Short Selling Rate, All Waiting for a Signature

Some people have seen a severely undervalued goldmine of computing power.

As of March 2026, out of the 12 institutions covering the company, 10 had given it a "Buy" rating with an average target price of $26.6. Investment banks, citing industry benchmarks, point out that the cost of building a new AI data center typically ranges from $8 million to $12 million per megawatt, suggesting that Bitdeer, with its low-cost electricity, has significant room for revaluation. However, another group cast a cold vote of dissent with their hard-earned money. In January 2026, KBW downgraded its rating to $14, and Zacks further issued a "Strong Sell" rating on March 19. The most direct data is that, as of March 27th, Bitdeer's short-selling positions accounted for 39.83% of its circulating shares. This means that for every 5 shares of circulating stock in the market, nearly 2 shares were lent out for short selling, with short sellers closely watching the uncertainties arising from their aggressive expansion. At this point, all the suspense and pressure finally boiled down to a single piece of paper—the contract for the AI ​​business. During the earnings call in February 2026, a core question repeatedly asked by analysts was: had a lease been signed for the 180 MW project in Norway? The CFO's response at the time was that they were in intensive negotiations with well-known companies in the industry. Their super blueprints have been laid out, and interest bills are being generated daily on schedule. Now, let's see who moves into that 180 megawatts.

data.title: AI Payment Protocol Research Status Report: The Emerging Two-Layer Architecture
data.description:
In the six months from September 2025 to March 2026, every major player in the global payments industry made significant moves. OpenAI and Stripe jointly released the Agentic Commerce Protocol (ACP). Google launched the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP). Within a week, Visa and Mastercard released their respective Agent payment frameworks. In the following two months, Coinbase's x402 protocol processed over 15 million transactions on the Base chain.

... In March 2026, Stripe and Tempo jointly released the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP). These coordinated moves by tech giants and financial institutions are no coincidence, but rather a collective response from the payment industry to the same challenge: as AI agents become the most active consumers on the internet, existing payment infrastructure is fundamentally inadequate to meet their operational needs. Every aspect of the traditional payment system is built on the premise of "human operation": relying on browser interfaces, manually filling out forms, clicking "confirm payment," and verifying identity via CAPTCHAs. The operational logic of agents is entirely different: it requires machine-readable standard interfaces, millisecond-level authorization responses, and infrastructure adapted to high-frequency, small-amount settlements. This battle for infrastructure will not be dominated by a single protocol, but is instead developing into a clear two-tiered architecture. The intent layer defines "who are the merchants and how to facilitate transactions," while the settlement layer defines "how funds actually flow." The two layers are independent and evolve independently, but neither is dispensable. Without either layer, the commercial closed loop of the Agent economy cannot be achieved.

Part One: Intent Orchestration Layer

The intent orchestration layer is responsible for transforming the agent's transaction intent into an executable end-to-end process: discovering goods or services, adding to cart, and triggering payment. This layer currently forms two distinctly different tracks.

1.1 Agent Shopping

The core contradiction in this field is not payment, but access. Traditional e-commerce platforms are designed for human users, and agents cannot parse visual pages or click interactive elements. To enable agents to complete purchases on behalf of humans, merchants must provide standardized, machine-readable interfaces.

ACP (Agentic Commerce Protocol): AI Shopping Experience within a Closed Ecosystem

ACP was jointly released by OpenAI and Stripe in September 2025.

ACP was jointly released by OpenAI and Stripe in September 2025.

Its core mechanism is delegated payment: when a user confirms a purchase, they grant payment authority to an Agent. The Agent completes the transaction using payment credentials that comply with the Delegated Payment Spec, while the merchant retains their Merchant of Record status. Currently, Stripe's SPT is the first and only implemented solution within this system. ChatGPT Instant Checkout launched in September 2025 but was shut down in March 2026 due to low conversion rates. OpenAI has shifted its strategic focus to product discovery, where ChatGPT displays products and drives users to the merchant's native site to complete the transaction. The ACP protocol itself is retained in a more streamlined form, supporting dedicated ChatGPT applications for several large retailers. Merchants need to apply to join, and OpenAI controls the display.

UCP (Universal Commerce Protocol): A Long-Term Strategy for Open Standards

UCP was personally announced by Google CEO Sundar Pichai at the NRF Retail Conference in January 2026. It has already gained more than 30 partners, covering mainstream platforms such as Shopify, Stripe, Visa, Mastercard, Walmart, and Wayfair.

The core mechanism of UCP is the merchant's capability declaration: merchants publish a JSON-formatted UCP configuration file under their own domain, declaring the transmission and payment capabilities they support, which AI can directly read. Google uses this to build Gemini as the core discovery layer for Agent Shopping. The key difference lies in Google's deliberate avoidance of the intermediary role. It doesn't need to own the transaction itself, only control the upstream link of product discovery. ACP and UCP are not competitors, but rather represent two different market propositions: the former uses a closed ecosystem to achieve an ultimate user experience and control, while the latter uses open standards to achieve greater scale and interoperability.

1.2 Transactions between Agents

If track A solves how agents complete shopping on behalf of humans, track B needs to solve a more fundamental problem: when both parties in a transaction are agents and no human merchants are involved, where does trust come from? Agents lack credibility endorsement, and consumer protection regulations are inapplicable. The core contradiction lies in: how to ensure the reliability of value exchange in a zero-trust environment.

If track A solves how agents complete shopping on behalf of humans, track B needs to solve a more fundamental problem: when both parties in a transaction are agents and no human merchants are involved, where does trust come from? Agents lack credibility endorsement, and consumer protection regulations are inapplicable.

  • Main Limitations： Its architecture relies on traditional card networks and is not suitable for high-frequency, low-value (e.g., less than 1 cent) machine-to-machine payment scenarios.

    • 2.2 Visa Intelligent Commerce and Mastercard Agentic Token

    • Core Idea： Upgrade the tokenization technology of traditional card organizations to adapt to agent transactions.

      • Operating Method: Dynamically encrypted payment tokens replace physical card numbers. Each token is bound to metadata such as the agent's identity, spending limit, validity period, and range of merchants eligible for transactions. Final fund clearing still occurs through the existing bank card network. Current Status: In September 2025, Mastercard partnered with the Commonwealth Bank of Australia to complete the world's first fully identity-verified agent transaction. Visa has also completed initial deployments in Europe through its "AI Ready" program.

    • Main Limitations：The inherent minimum transaction fees of bank card networks constitute a hard constraint, making it difficult to support the potentially massive volume of micropayments below $1 in the future.

    2.3 x402 (Coinbase)

    • Core Idea: Returning to the underlying internet protocol, we utilize the "402 Paid Request" status code, which is not widely used in the HTTP standard, to natively integrate payments.

    • How it Works: When an Agent requests a resource that requires payment, the server returns a 402 response along with payment parameters. After the agent signs and authorizes, the settlement node within the protocol completes the atomic swap on the blockchain (usually using USDC), taking approximately two seconds. The entire process requires no account registration, API keys, or authentication. Data and Status Quo: As of the end of 2025, the protocol had processed over 100 million transactions across multiple blockchains. However, some analysts point out that a significant portion of this traffic consists of internal testing and recurring transactions. While its architecture allows for denominated payment designs and has no minimum transaction fee limit, the current challenge lies in increasing its adoption density and transaction quality in real-world business scenarios.

    2.4 Nanopayments (Circle)

    • Core Idea: As an enhancement to x402, it is specifically optimized for the economic model of extremely high-frequency, extremely small-amount payment scenarios.

    Core Idea: As an enhancement to x402, it is specifically optimized for the economic model of extremely high-frequency, extremely small-amount payment scenarios.

    Operating Mechanism: It also triggers an HTTP 402 response, but uses a batch processing architecture at the settlement layer: the payer first deposits USDC into the Circle gateway, and subsequent payments are authorized via off-chain signatures, with on-chain settlements completed periodically in batches. This reduces gas costs to negligible levels, supporting payments as low as one millionth of a dollar. Key Limitations: Both parties to the transaction must pre-open an account and deposit funds into the Circle gateway, making it a semi-closed system to some extent, and preventing real-time atomic fund transfers. The protocol launched its testnet in March 2026.

    2.5 MPP (Tempo + Stripe)

    • Core Idea: Build a unified, pluggable multi-track payment framework, and apply to be the "official implementation solution" for HTTP 402. Core Innovations: It allows developers to integrate multiple payment tracks within the same protocol framework, with agents able to choose as needed during transactions: Tempo Stablecoin Track: Supports on-chain settlement for individual transactions or off-chain session batch settlement. Stripe Fiat Track: Completes bank card payments via shared payment tokens.

    • Direct Access to Card Organizations: Directly uses Visa/Mastercard smart tokens.

    • Bitcoin Lightning Network: Integrated via Lightspark.

    • Key Features：MPP introduces the concept of "payment sessions," similar to OAuth authorization. After an Agent pre-authorizes and tops up once, seamless, continuous, real-time payments can be made within a session without requiring every transaction to be recorded on the blockchain.

    • Strategic Significance: Stripe plays a dual role here—it is both a co-developer of the protocol and a payment option provided within the protocol. This means that regardless of whether the market ultimately leans towards the open HTTP 402 system or traditional fiat currency channels, Stripe can ensure that its core payment business is embedded in the future ecosystem.

    Part Three: Current Situation, Challenges and Opportunities

    3.1 Current Situation and Challenges

    Over the past six months, all relevant core protocols have been launched, but the overall commercialization process has lagged behind.

    In the past six months, all relevant core protocols have been launched, but the overall commercialization process has lagged behind.

    At the settlement layer, x402 leads in transaction volume, but its actual daily average commercial transaction value is approximately $28,000. At the orchestration layer, the core ACP product was shut down due to excessively low conversion rates. New protocols such as ERC-8183 and MPP generally face a situation where the narrative precedes implementation. This marks a critical stage: the protocol architecture is basically complete, but large-scale commercial applications have not yet started. The current core challenge is the fragmentation of the intent orchestration layer. Merchants need to deal with multiple independent standards, SDKs, and compliance processes simultaneously, leading to high integration costs and unclear expectations. History shows that fragmented markets eventually give rise to a unified integration layer, but this situation may be different: platforms that control traffic entry points (such as OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft) have a strong incentive to build and maintain their own closed ecosystems, rather than promoting open integration. This logic is playing out simultaneously in the global market, and the final landscape is likely to be the coexistence of multiple regional closed ecosystems, rather than a unified open standard. Therefore, the future integration layer will not be dominated by the platform, but built by third-party infrastructure providers serving merchants. 3.2 Market Opportunities Based on the above judgment, opportunities clearly exist at two levels: Settlement Layer: The Most Certain Opportunity Regardless of how fragmented the upper-layer ecosystem is, payment is a fundamental problem that any agent must solve. A clear trend is that the orchestration layer continues to fragment due to platform interests, while the settlement layer is moving towards abstraction and integration due to developers' efficiency pressures. Developers cannot maintain independent payment integrations for each ecosystem, and the economic impetus for integration towards a unified solution is increasing. This places clear demands on Agent wallets: they must support multiple payment tracks. Fiat currency tracks (such as SPT and Agentic Token) cover traditional physical goods consumption, while stablecoin tracks (such as x402 and MPP Session) cover on-chain services and A2A transactions. Both scenarios coexist and will not merge in the short term. The responsibility for flexible adaptation lies with the Agent, not the merchant: merchants choose which payment tracks to support, a relatively stable decision for them; businesses only need to configure stablecoins and authorization cards for the Agent, and the Agent can complete payments according to the tracks supported by the counterparty. Only wallets capable of handling multiple tracks simultaneously can cover the Agent's complete consumption scenarios. Its value will continuously accumulate as each transaction crosses different ecosystems, forming a deep infrastructure moat. A2A Economy and Business Model Restructuring: A Long-Term Blue Ocean Direction

    The real market gap lies in application-layer services. Currently, the A2A economy is still limited to native cryptocurrency scenarios. Technically, it's entirely feasible for an agent to "hire" another agent to complete real-world tasks (such as data analysis, content creation, legal research, and code review), but the corresponding supply of per-call API services is extremely scarce. This is the biggest long-term opportunity and also the direction with the least competition.

    This opportunity is currently constrained by a real cold-start dilemma: reputation-based trust mechanisms like ERC-8183 require sufficient transaction density to generate meaningful trust signals.

    Microsoft predicts that the number of active AI agents will reach 1.3 billion by 2028, while the current number is orders of magnitude lower than this target. This is not a temporary gap that will naturally disappear, but a hurdle that the A2A economy must overcome to expand beyond crypto. Its deeper significance lies in the restructuring of business models. The mainstream advertising and subscription models on the internet are based on the assumption that "users are people." Agents are not swayed by advertising, nor do they need monthly subscriptions; they only pay for the results of a single task. The "pay-per-call" model, represented by HTTP 402, provides API service providers with a new path: shifting from selling access permissions to selling exact results, achieving a more refined value exchange. The expansion of the A2A economy and the adoption of HTTP 402 are essentially two sides of the same coin. Conclusion Agent-based commerce will evolve along two parallel paths: On the consumer side (Agents shopping on behalf of others), development will primarily rely on credit cards, depending on the establishment of enterprise authorization and user trust; on the agent-to-agent (A2A) side, technology is ready on the stablecoin track, awaiting the scaling of applications and services. The final landscape will be the collaborative evolution of a two-layer protocol stack: the intent orchestration layer determines how transactions occur, and the settlement layer ensures how value flows. For builders, the key now is to construct broad access and integration capabilities. Infrastructure that can automatically route cross-protocol transactions and shield developers from underlying complexities will gain a structural advantage when the market explodes. This is a value that accumulates silently and is difficult to replace. The key to triggering the inflection point will be the moment when enterprises are willing to delegate spending authority to agents—including auditable transaction tracking, budget delegation mechanisms, and clear accountability in case of agent mispurchases. At that time, agent wallets that simultaneously cover multiple payment tracks and easy-to-use "pay-per-use" service catalogs will become the two most critical and yet-to-be-occupied pieces of infrastructure. Currently, neither position has a strong competitor, and both will become crucial at the same moment.

data.title: An epic leak: 512,000 lines of Claude Code source code have been open-sourced.
data.description:
Author: Yang Chen, Wall Street Insights

Anthropic suffered what can be described as the largest code leak in the industry. The complete source code of Claude Code was completely exposed due to a low-level packaging error. Over 510,000 lines of TypeScript code, more than 40 utility modules, and several unreleased core functions were thus exposed to developers worldwide.

This was an accident, but also a warning.

... While this leak did not affect Claude's core model weights or user data, it fully exposed the internal architecture logic of Claude Code, its system prompt design, and tool invocation mechanisms, also revealing several unreleased features and potential security logic to the public. Industry insiders believe this incident will substantially lower the knowledge threshold for AI Agent engineering and accelerate the competitive evolution of the entire developer ecosystem. It is worth noting that this is not the first time Anthropic has made such a mistake. In February 2025, an earlier version of the company's Claude Code was exposed due to a similar source map oversight. This latest leak further raises questions about the maturity of the software supply chain security of this AI star company valued at over $18 billion. A .map file triggers the discovery of 510,000 lines of code. Chaofan Shou, a researcher at blockchain security company Fuzzland, first exposed this incident on X. Anthropic's official npm package, @anthropic-ai/claude-code version 2.1.88, unexpectedly contained a 60MB file named cli.js.map. Within the cli.js.map file, there are two key arrays: sources (a list of file paths) and sourcesContent (the corresponding complete source code content), with a one-to-one index. This means that anyone can extract all the original code simply by downloading this JSON file, making the operation extremely easy. According to analysis, the source map file contains a total of 4,756 source files, of which 1,906 are Claude Code's own TypeScript/TSX source files, and the remaining 2,850 are node_modules dependencies. The total code size exceeds 512,000 lines. Within hours of the incident being exposed, the number of stars on the mirror repository on GitHub rapidly exceeded 5,000. Anthropic has removed the source map from the npm package. However, earlier versions of the npm package have been archived by various parties, and related content continues to circulate in the developer community. The complete architecture is revealed for the first time. The restored source code provides the most complete view of the Claude Code architecture to date. The code shows that Claude Code uses the React and Ink frameworks to build its terminal interface, runs on the Bun runtime, and its core is a REPL loop. It supports natural language input and slash commands, and interacts with the LLM API through a tool system at the underlying level. At the tool level, the code contains over 40 independent modules, covering file reading and writing, Bash command execution, LSP protocol integration, and sub-agent generation capabilities, forming a fully functional "all-purpose toolbox." At the reasoning level, a core file named QueryEngine.ts has a staggering 46,000 lines of code, handling all the work of reasoning logic processing, token counting, and the "thought chain" loop. At the multi-agent level, the leaked code reveals a coordinator module and a bridge module. The latter is responsible for connecting mainstream IDEs such as VS Code and JetBrains, demonstrating that Claude Code possesses engineering capabilities for multi-machine collaboration and deep embedding into development environments.

Unreleased Features Unexpectedly Revealed

Among the leaked features, perhaps the most attention-grabbing are several never-before-released functionalities.

The mode codenamed Kairos is one of the most eye-catching. The code reveals that this is an autonomous daemon process with a persistent lifecycle, supporting background sessions and memory integration, meaning that Claude can act as a persistent background AI agent, continuously processing tasks and accumulating understanding of the project.

Another electronic pet system, known as the "Buddy System," is embedded in the code, containing 18 species, rarity levels, shiny variants, and attribute statistics—a design clearly born from the playful spirit of Anthropic engineers, existing alongside the core architecture in the codebase. At the mode design level, the code also reveals "Coordinator Mode," allowing Claude to schedule subordinate agents running in parallel, and "Auto Mode," an AI classifier that automatically approves tool permissions, designed to simplify the operation confirmation process. Furthermore, a feature named "Undercover Mode" has sparked controversy—according to the code description, this mode automatically activates when Anthropic employees operate in the public warehouse, erasing AI-related traces from submission records, and cannot be manually turned off. Security researchers point out that although this leak did not directly involve model weights or user privacy data, the potential risks cannot be ignored. The leaked content fully exposes the internal security logic and may reveal attack vectors such as Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), providing a starting point for subsequent security research. The open-source community has begun exploring forked versions based on the leaked code and attempting to integrate them with other proxy frameworks. In terms of industry background, npm is the world's largest JavaScript package repository, processing millions of downloads daily. This packaging error serves as a reminder to companies that while pursuing rapid release schedules, they must strengthen source file review mechanisms within their CI/CD pipelines. The direct warning to all developers releasing npm packages is: always check that the .map file is included in the release before publishing. A single line of the sourcesContent field is enough to expose the complete source code. The Agent ecosystem may be approaching an acceleration inflection point. From an industry impact perspective, the significance of this event may transcend a technical accident itself. The unexpected public disclosure of the complete engineering implementation of a top-tier AI Agent will significantly lower the knowledge barrier in this field. Developers can directly refer to Claude Code's architecture design, prompt logic, and tool invocation mechanisms for learning and reference, shortening the exploration cycle of independent research and development. Meanwhile, this incident also unexpectedly confirms Anthropic's technological accumulation in agent engineering—both the multi-agent coordination mechanism and the design of the persistent background daemon process demonstrate an engineering depth that surpasses similar products. Claude Code, as an extension tool of the Anthropic ecosystem, is primarily aimed at professional developers and competes with AI coding assistants such as GitHub Copilot and Cursor. Whether this source code release can, against the backdrop of intensified competition, accelerate collective innovation in AI agent architecture within the industry is being closely watched by the industry's subsequent response.

data.title: 2026 Q1 Review: Traditional Assets Offer 24/7 Trading, Crypto Market Undergoes Restructuring
data.description:
Author: Tanay Ved, Senior Researcher at CoinMetrics; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • Amidst a volatile macroeconomic and geopolitical environment, the crypto market continues to face pressure, but ETF demand has gradually improved this quarter, providing support for Bitcoin's current price level.

  • On-chain trading venues and asset tokenization are further driving traditional assets into the 24/7 trading market. The launch of stock and index perpetual contracts on platforms such as Hyperliquid, as well as the addition of new stock perpetual products on mainstream exchanges, are driving steady growth in holdings.

  • On-chain trading venues and asset tokenization are further promoting the entry of traditional assets into the 24/7 trading market. The stock and index perpetual contracts launched by platforms such as Hyperliquid, and the new stock perpetual products added by mainstream exchanges, are driving steady growth in holdings.

    • The total supply of stablecoins has remained stable at around $300 billion, with adjusted on-chain transaction volume rising to approximately $21.5 trillion in the first quarter of 2026. Meanwhile, regulatory policies regarding stablecoin yields and issuance are becoming increasingly clear in various countries, continuously influencing the industry's development landscape. The first quarter of 2026 is drawing to a close, making it an opportune time to analyze the development trajectory and core themes of the crypto market. The market in the first quarter of 2026 was affected by multiple uncertainties arising from geopolitics and the macroeconomy, resulting in increased volatility and a decline in risk appetite. Despite the challenges facing the crypto market as a whole, with a total market capitalization decline of approximately 22%, highlights emerged in areas such as tokenized stocks and 24/7 on-chain trading of traditional asset classes, and continued progress was made in industry infrastructure. This article will review the first quarter of 2026, analyzing the market trends and core themes that influenced the quarter. Market Performance Bitcoin (BTC) has fallen more than 30% from its February high of around $95,000, and has fallen 22% year-to-date in the first quarter. **In addition to macroeconomic pressures, the widespread sell-off of risk assets and the liquidation of derivatives markets further exacerbated the decline, and also reignited discussions about Bitcoin's safe-haven attributes and store-of-value function.** However, **since the outbreak of the conflict in Iran on February 28, Bitcoin has shown significant relative strength compared to stocks and gold, demonstrating a certain resilience, and market demand has also recovered somewhat.**

    Source: Coin Metrics price and Google Finance

    In the crypto asset market, price movements are mainly concentrated on a few altcoins with strong narrative appeal and real-world usage growth, indicating a continued narrowing of market breadth.

    Cryptocurrencies that significantly outperformed the market include Hyperliquid (HYPE), Bittensor (TAO), and Morpho (MORPHO), all of which saw quarterly gains exceeding 30%. Hyperliquid benefited from the growth of its HIP-3 market (particularly in commodities and stock index categories), further expanding its business from the crypto space to other asset classes. Bittensor and Morpho, on the other hand, benefited from the development of artificial intelligence infrastructure and decentralized finance lending, respectively, with institutional funds showing continued interest in decentralized AI and smart vault management.

    Source: Coin Metrics Prices & Network Data Pro

    Bitcoin Demand Gradually Stabilizes

    The risk aversion sentiment prevalent in the early part of this quarter reversed in March. Although the market still showed some weakness, demand for Bitcoin spot ETFs has improved significantly, reversing the continuous outflow of funds since November 2025.

    ... On a 30-day rolling cycle, ETF net inflows have turned positive, exceeding 30,000 Bitcoins, providing support for Bitcoin's consolidation around $70,000.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    24/7 On-Chain Markets and Tokenized Stocks

    Hyperliquid and Traditional Asset Classes

    A major trend this year is the accelerated integration of traditional financial markets with on-chain infrastructure through tokenization and 24/7 trading models. The growth of perpetual contracts in traditional asset classes is one of the most direct manifestations of this trend. Hyperliquid launched the HIP-3 market, covering stocks, indices, and commodities, and its non-crypto asset trading volume increased significantly to approximately 45% this quarter. Amid geopolitical tensions, traders sought 24/7 exposure to precious metals and crude oil, resulting in a significant increase in the platform's overall trading volume and open interest (OI). HIP-3 traditional asset classes accounted for approximately 28% of the platform's total OI, amounting to $1.9 billion.

    Source: Coin Metrics Market Data Feed

    The Rise of Stock Perpetual Contracts

    In this space, as trading platforms continue to expand their service offerings, perpetual contracts for mainstream individual stocks and indices have become the fastest-growing category. Kraken launched xStocks stock perpetual futures contracts in February, and Coinbase International also launched stock perpetual products, providing investors with leveraged exposure to US stocks. Meanwhile, [XYZ], Hyperliquid's largest HIP-3 trading platform provider, partnered with S&P Dow Jones Indices to launch the first official S&P 500 perpetual contract, further enriching its trading market matrix for global equities. The open interest in stock and index perpetual contracts on the Hyperliquid platform is steadily increasing. Core index products such as XYZ100 (Nasdaq 100) and S&P 500 have now become among the largest trading categories on the platform in terms of holdings, while individual stock contracts such as NVIDIA (NVDA) and Micron Technology (MU) have also achieved considerable market depth. Simultaneously, the issuance of tokenized stocks and funds is growing in tandem, from structures like xStocks to tokenized money market funds and equity funds issued by institutions like Ondo on Ethereum and Solana, all continuing to expand. This series of growths in tokenized stocks and Real-World Asset (RWA) perpetual contracts further confirms a trend: On-chain trading venues are gradually becoming an all-weather extension of traditional financial markets, rather than being limited to crypto-native trading scenarios. Stablecoins: Stable Supply, Increased Utility Stablecoins continue to play a cornerstone role in on-chain liquidity. Despite the overall market sell-off, the total supply of stablecoins remained stable at around $300 billion in the first quarter of 2026, with a slight rebound in the 30-day rolling supply growth rate. The most notable growth was seen in USDS, a USD stablecoin issued by Sky Protocol (formerly MakerDAO) and backed by crypto assets and real-world assets (RWA). USDS supply increased by 43%, reaching approximately $8 billion; USDC, issued by Circle, saw its supply rise to $77 billion, while USDT remained stable at around $184 billion.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    Despite stable supply, stablecoins have seen significant increases in both velocity of circulation and usage. The adjusted total stablecoin transaction volume reached $21.5 trillion this quarter, approximately three times the level of the first quarter of 2025. Over 80% of this transaction volume came from USDC, and its share of transaction usage is gradually expanding compared to USDT.

    This activity is primarily driven by USDC on the Base chain, which alone saw $13 trillion in transactions in Q1. As we analyzed in a recent report, while end-user payment and settlement scenarios are also growing, a significant portion of these fund flows still reflects DeFi infrastructure activity, such as liquidity provider (LP) rebalancing and flash loans. Looking ahead, the industry's development direction may depend on stablecoin yield and distribution mechanisms. The newly released draft of the **CLARITY Act** proposes prohibiting passive holding of stablecoin balances for yield, but allowing activity-based rewards linked to payments or platform usage. This provision could change the profit model of key participants. For Coinbase, stablecoin-related revenue currently accounts for more than 25% of its total revenue. Restricting its ability to offer USDC yields would weaken its ability to attract and retain funds. Circle, on the other hand, would be less affected. If the high-interest-rate environment and clearer rules drive use cases towards payments and transaction-based revenue, Circle might even benefit. As the bill progresses, its ripple effects on DeFi lending, interest-bearing stablecoins, and tokenized government bonds deserve close attention. The US SEC also released a digital asset classification framework this quarter, providing important and clear guidance at the regulatory level. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) jointly released an explanatory document outlining a five-tiered framework for digital assets and clarifying how existing securities and commodities regulations apply to each type of asset. **Digital Commodities:** Core public blockchain native tokens, whose value primarily relies on functional crypto networks and market supply and demand (such as mainstream Layer 1 public blockchain tokens), are classified as commodities rather than securities. **Digital Collectibles and Tools:** NFTs, in-game items, gas fee tokens, and permission tokens are generally not considered securities unless they are fragmented or primarily marketed as investment products. Payment-based stablecoins: Payment-based stablecoins backed by fiat currency and real-world assets (RWA) are considered money-like instruments, but those with yield attributes or ineligible designs may still be classified as securities. Digital securities: Tokenized stocks, bonds, credit-based real-world assets, and similar instruments, regardless of their on-chain form, are all securities. Staking, mining, and encapsulation services: Native staking, mining, airdrops, and underlying asset encapsulation are not securities transactions, but aggregate staking, yield-encapsulated, or structured tokens that promise returns to investors may be considered investment contracts. Conclusion While asset prices remain highly sensitive to macroeconomic and geopolitical factors, the underlying infrastructure of the industry continues to improve. Bitcoin has gradually found support at current price levels, and on-chain trading platforms are further penetrating the 24/7 trading arena for stocks, commodities, and real-world assets (RWAs). Meanwhile, traditional financial giants such as the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) and Nasdaq are actively developing asset tokenization, driving the modernization of stock trading systems. The progress of the Clarity Act and regulatory policies related to stablecoin yields will be key factors influencing industry development. If the macroeconomic environment continues to improve, market risk appetite is expected to gradually return to the crypto asset sector.

data.title: Blockchain games lose to reality; Web3 doesn't believe in dreams.
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

Recently, Lily Liu, President of the Solana Foundation, posted on X that "games on the blockchain will not return," and stated that blockchain games are dead.

Her judgment stemmed from a Polymarket post, "Mark Zuckerberg's Meta is gradually abandoning its metaverse vision after spending $80 billion." While Meta's blueprint didn't explicitly involve blockchain or crypto assets, its strategy highly overlapped with the future depicted by Web3 blockchain games in recent years: virtual worlds, digital asset ownership, and immersive online economies. Even the wealthiest players are quitting. Was blockchain gaming, once considered the most promising narrative for the crypto industry to "break out," now facing its demise? The collapse of the entire sector: blockchain game projects shutting down one after another? Last August, ofPlay issued an announcement that sounded like a confession to the market, stating that its on-chain pirate RPG, *Pirate Nation*, would be shut down within 30 days. Two dedicated blockchains were taken offline, token rewards were reduced to zero, and community players could only burn their assets to obtain so-called "certificates," which might one day be useful, but most likely wouldn't. This game studio had raised $33 million two years prior, vowing to build the future of on-chain games. Following the announcement, the PIRATE token plummeted by 92% within days. Co-founder Adam Fern admitted, "Shutting down Pirate Nation was one of the toughest decisions I've ever been involved in. But the truth is, it could never have been a groundbreaking mass-market title." Pirate Nation is not an isolated case; it's just a small part of the massive collapse of blockchain games in 2025. Let's take a look at the list of blockchain games that announced their closure last year. Ember Sword, an Ethereum game that attracted $203 million through NFT land purchases, announced its closure last May, with developer Bright Star Studios stating frankly that it lacked funds. Nyan Heroes, a third-person shooter battle royale game built on Solana, was on the wish lists of over 250,000 PC gamers, but it also shut down last May due to a funding crisis, with its token NYAN plummeting by over 99% from its peak. Symbiogenesis, an Ethereum-based game from Square Enix, the creators of Final Fantasy, also ended in July. Gala Games' MMORPG, officially licensed from The Walking Dead, also shut down in July. MetalCore, a mechanized combat game based on NFTs, disappeared without a trace after shutting down its servers in March; the developer has quietly moved on to releasing a new game on Steam that has nothing to do with blockchain. The most recent disappointing development in the market is Wildcard. After its TGE in March of this year, the project's market capitalization only reached a peak of $1.1 million, with the community widely questioning its irresponsibility and soft power. According to crypto asset data platform RootData, Wildcard previously raised $46 million in funding, led by Paradigm.

Its founder, Paul Bettner, was involved in the development of well-known games such as "Words With Friends" and "Lucky's Tale," but now, even with the backing of top-tier VCs and the management of veteran game developers, it couldn't stop the collapse of the entire blockchain game sector. Besides these, there are also projects like *Deadrop*, *Blast Royale*, *Mojo Melee*, *Tokyo Beast*, *OpenSeason*, and *Captain Tsubasa Rivals*. Behind each of these projects lies millions or even tens of millions of dollars in investment, the accumulation of countless game users, and ultimately, promises that vanished. Web2 players want a good game, but Web3 players only want profits. Most of the founders have real game development backgrounds, and their vision for on-chain games during fundraising wasn't entirely empty talk. Why then do these projects ultimately end up shutting down or reverting to Web2? "Web3 games have already built an entire investor-driven capital structure through tokens and NFTs before player demand has even been validated." In other words, the people who fund these games are not the same people who will ultimately remain in the game. When development reveals that the on-chain player base is smaller than expected and more inclined towards short-term arbitrage, token prices continue to fall, and development costs keep rising, the studios are left with only two choices: shut down or abandon their blockchain identity and move to the traditional market. Regardless of which path they take, early Web3 investors and NFT holders are the ultimate payers. The farming simulation game *Moonfrost* is a typical example. Developer Oxalis Games raised $6.5 million and ran a Play-to-Airdrop campaign for over a year, selling 1,833 NFT boxes at $150 each. Then, in November 2025, the team announced their departure from Web3, relaunching on Steam as a paid PC game, without NFTs, tokens, or blockchain. Just the day before the announcement, CEO Ric Moore was publicly discussing how to build "slow but meaningful Web3 games." The team's reasoning was: "Web3 players want to make money, Web2 players just want a good game." They spent three years and millions of dollars to understand the real rules. A 2025 industry report by the Blockchain Game Alliance (BGA) also confirmed the decline of blockchain games: annual investment in blockchain games dropped to approximately $293 million, a dramatic decrease compared to $4 billion in 2021 and a peak of $10 billion in 2022. DWF Labs described the current phase as a "necessary reset." Perhaps the biggest consequence of this sector's failure is a crisis of public trust in the entire blockchain game industry. The BGA report shows that 36% of respondents listed "fraud, deception, or rug pulls" as the biggest threat to the industry. Even though the closure of most projects was not intentional fraud, from an outsider's perspective, the recurring cycle of "fundraising, token issuance, and collapse" is almost indistinguishable from a rug pull. "This industry needs both genuine game developers and genuine users who want to play games; neither can be lacking." Infrastructure and market conditions are advantages, stablecoins and AI bring new opportunities. The collapse of the blockchain game narrative does not mean the end of consumer-grade applications in the crypto industry. A BGA report shows that 65.8% of industry practitioners remain optimistic about the next 12 months, an optimism based on deliverable products and sustainable revenue models. Meanwhile, the massive transaction volumes handled by stablecoins and AI tools reducing game development costs to a fraction of what they used to be demonstrate that the infrastructure and market conditions have never disappeared. In fact, several possible paths can be seen from the perspectives of many developers. NEXPACE CEO Sunyoung Hwang, when discussing their game *MapleStory Universe*, emphasized a core principle: wallets, gas fees, and token economics are obstacles, not advantages, for most players. The blockchain layer should do meaningful work behind the scenes, such as enabling true asset ownership and driving an open economy, while players should focus solely on the game itself. "If the operation of the infrastructure permeates the game experience, the game design has failed." Animoca Brands CEO Robby Yung and PLAY Network CEO Christina Macedo, on the other hand, believe that retention rate is the only true measure of success. Retention data at D1, D7, and D30 levels holds true in the console era, in the mobile gaming era, and remains true in the crypto industry. Macedo points out that the standard benchmarks for mobile games are 35-45% D1 retention, 15-25% D7, and 5-10% D30, while most Web3 games don't even reach these basic health metrics. Gabby Dizon, co-founder of Yield Guild Games, believes the industry's failure stems from "spending too much time measuring the wrong things," including using outdated metrics such as VC funding, token prices, and NFT sales. The real metric is simply that players are willing to pay because they see value in the gaming experience. Finally, there are the opportunities brought by stablecoins and AI. The BGA report indicates that over a quarter of respondents consider stablecoins key to the industry's success. Compared to volatile game tokens, stablecoins are more user-friendly and easier to understand for new users, and are increasingly being used for tournament prizes, in-game rewards, and cross-border payments. Sequence further points out that savvy game developers are focusing on stablecoin payments, whether for on-chain assets or other scenarios, as lower fees, instant settlement, and easier revenue sharing offer significant advantages. Furthermore, AI is changing cost structures. Simon Davis of Mighty Bear Games points out that AI-native teams are surpassing the output of traditional studios with a fraction of the cost and manpower. Animoca Brands also believes that the key to sustainability in 2026 lies in AI-driven or AI-assisted development practices, which will fundamentally change the economic model for creating high-quality game content. Blockchain games aren't dead yet; is this a necessary reset? The core contradiction of the past blockchain game cycle has remained unchanged: investor-driven capital structures have outpaced the validation of player demand. When retention rates cannot support the token economy, and development costs devour funding figures, the only end result for projects is shutdown or de-blockchainization, with early holders always bearing the brunt. However, this reshuffling has also led to a more pragmatic consensus among game developers: making blockchain invisible, measuring success by retention rates rather than token prices, using stablecoins instead of high-volatility tokens as the payment layer, and leveraging AI to restructure development costs. The common thread among these directions is: first, create a game that can withstand the test of traditional market metrics, and then allow blockchain to realize its true value at the underlying level. Blockchain games may not be dead as Lily Liu claims, but the market is indeed moving away from the old cycle of driving user numbers with tokens, until development funds are exhausted and ultimately only Web2 games remain.

data.title: The source code for Claude Code has been leaked and open-sourced, and it's going viral online.
data.description:
The AI ​​community has gone crazy! Just now, an X account named Chaofan Shou posted that "Claude Code's source code has been leaked through a mapping file in its npm registry!" In a short time, the post has garnered millions of views. A large number of netizens have quickly followed up, frantically forking the project and analyzing its source code. Link: https://github.com/sanbuphy/claude-code-source-code Some netizens commented, "This seems to be an accident by the Claude Code team who accidentally uploaded the source code of v2.1.88 directly to the npm package. The overall code structure is very mature, and the entire repo..." The process is very detailed, with the main workflow including REPL startup, QueryEngine, tool registration, Slash commands, permission system, task system, and multi-layered state management—a very typical production-grade AI agent harness design. All tools are available for viewing.
